---
title: "Chapter 1: What is Data?"
subtitle: "Session 1: Data All Around Us — Capturing and Using Everyday Data"
format: html
---

## Big Questions
:::{.callout-note}
* **What is data?**
* **Where does it come from in our lives?**
:::

## 1.1 Data All Around Us

[cite_start]From the moment you wake up to the time you go to sleep, nearly every action you take can produce data[cite: 106]. [cite_start]Think about how much information you generate in a single day[cite: 105]. [cite_start]Whether you are checking your phone, walking to class, streaming music, or scrolling through social media, you are leaving a digital trail[cite: 107].

### Sensors and Sources
Most of this data is captured automatically by sensors embedded in the devices we use every day.
* [cite_start]**Smartphones:** Your phone’s step counter tracks your physical movement[cite: 108].
* [cite_start]**Web Browsers:** Your history logs your searches and interests[cite: 108].
* [cite_start]**Apps:** Services like Spotify or Apple Music save your favorite songs and create playlists based on your listening habits[cite: 108].
* **GPS:** Tracks your location data to help with navigation or tag your photos.

## 1.2 Types of Data: Structured vs. Unstructured

As we explore the data generated by these devices, it is helpful to categorize it into two main types:

1.  **Structured Data:** This is highly organized and formatted data that is easily searchable in relational databases.
    * *Examples:* Your daily step count (a number), dates on a calendar, or an Excel spreadsheet of expenses.
2.  **Unstructured Data:** This data has no pre-defined format or organization, making it more difficult to collect, process, and analyze.
    * *Examples:* The body of a text message, video files, photos, or social media posts.

---

## 1.3 Activity 1: Log Your Data

**Objective:** To become aware of the sheer volume of data generated in a typical day.

**Instructions:**
[cite_start]For this activity, take one full day and write down everything you did yesterday[cite: 107].
1.  [cite_start]**List Actions:** Include things like checking your phone, walking to class, or using social media[cite: 107].
2.  [cite_start]**Identify Data:** Beside each action, identify what data is created[cite: 108].
    * *Example:* Walking $\rightarrow$ Step count data.
    * [cite_start]*Example:* Searching Google $\rightarrow$ Browser history logs[cite: 108].
3.  [cite_start]**Locate Storage:** Try to be specific about where this data is stored (e.g., on your device, in the cloud, or in an app)[cite: 109].

:::{.callout-tip title="Reflection"}
* [cite_start]What did the data help you understand that you couldn’t see before? [cite: 111]
* [cite_start]Did anything surprise you? [cite: 112]
* [cite_start]How might your data be incomplete or biased? [cite: 113]
:::

---

## 1.4 Activity 2: Explore Your Google Data

[cite_start]**Objective:** To visualize the depth of personal data companies collect about you[cite: 115].

**Instructions:**
1.  Go to **Google Takeout**.
2.  [cite_start]Browse through the available sections to see what is stored—such as Search history, YouTube watch history, Calendar events, location data, and app activity[cite: 116].
3.  [cite_start]You do not need to download the data; just observing the categories is enough[cite: 117].
4.  [cite_start]Pay attention to details like timestamps, specific locations, and topics[cite: 118].

:::{.callout-tip title="Reflection"}
* [cite_start]What types of data surprised you? [cite: 121]
* [cite_start]How detailed is the information? [cite: 122]
* [cite_start]Notice how much your online habits reveal about your interests, routines, and even your personality[cite: 119].
* [cite_start]How could someone else use this data if they had access to it? [cite: 123]
:::

---

## 1.5 Activity 3: Text Message Analysis

[cite_start]**Objective:** To analyze your own communication patterns using data[cite: 125].

**Instructions:**
[cite_start]Choose a recent text message conversation and record the following structured data[cite: 125]:
* [cite_start]Number of messages sent vs. received[cite: 126].
* [cite_start]Average time between responses[cite: 127].
* [cite_start]The most frequently used words or emojis[cite: 128].

**Sentiment Analysis:**
[cite_start]Next, use a **Sentiment Analysis Tool** to analyze the tone of your messages[cite: 129]. [cite_start]Copy and paste a short section of text into the tool to see if it identifies the tone as positive, negative, or neutral[cite: 130].

:::{.callout-tip title="Reflection"}
* [cite_start]**Communication Balance:** Who tends to start conversations more often—you or your friend? [cite: 133]
* [cite_start]**Message Length:** Are your messages shorter or longer than your friend’s? [cite: 134]
* [cite_start]**Relationship Insights:** What might this say about your relationship? [cite: 135]
* **Machine Interpretation:** Think about how the automated tool compared to your actual intent. [cite_start]How could a computer misread your tone (e.g., sarcasm or jokes)? [cite: 131, 136]
:::

---

## 1.6 Activity 4: Pose Detection & Computer Vision

[cite_start]**Objective:** To understand how machines (AI) interpret physical movement and unstructured data[cite: 138].

**Instructions:**
We will use a computer vision tool (like [Teachable Machine](https://teachablemachine.withgoogle.com/)) to detect movements.
1.  [cite_start]Open the pose-detection demo and allow your camera to detect your body[cite: 138].
2.  [cite_start]**Move:** Try raising your arms, turning sideways, or stepping out of the frame[cite: 139].
3.  [cite_start]**Test Limits:** Move faster or slower to see how accurately the computer tracks you[cite: 140].
4.  [cite_start]**Experiment:** Change lighting or camera angles to see what affects detection quality[cite: 141].

:::{.callout-important title="Key Concept"}
[cite_start]This activity demonstrates how AI vision systems work and where they struggle—a core concept in machine learning[cite: 142].
:::

:::{.callout-tip title="Reflection"}
* [cite_start]What happened when you moved quickly or out of frame? [cite: 144]
* [cite_start]What situations might cause the pose detector to fail? [cite: 145]
* [cite_start]How could pose detection be helpful in real-life situations (e.g., fitness apps, accessibility tools, or security)? [cite: 146]
:::
